complex_text: Russian scrambling, or constituent reordering, while receiving less attention than scrambling in, say, German or Japanese, has been investigated by a number of researchers . Here, we discuss possible derivations of both SVO and OVS sentences containing quantifiers in subject and object position, and the corresponding consequences for scope interpretation. We assume that in principle, the scope configuration can be affected by reconstruction as well as by covert QR. In this section, we lay out the logical possibilities of how inverse scope can be derived via reconstruction or via covert QR. In section 2.3, we will address how these possibilities relate to the role of IS in Russian.
simplified_text: Russian sentence structure, or how words are arranged in a sentence, is important to study. Many researchers have looked into how Russian sentences are structured. Here, we'll talk about how sentences with special words (quantifiers) in the subject and object positions can be arranged in different ways, and how this affects how we understand the sentence. We think that the way the sentence is structured can be changed in two ways: one is by using a hidden rule, and the other is by using a special rule that is not spoken. In this section, we'll explain the different ways this can happen. Later, we'll discuss how these ways relate to the role of a special word (IS) in Russian.

complex_text: Neeleman and Titov propose that, in Russian, long-distance movement of a contrastively focused element across clause boundaries results in obligatory reconstruction for scope, for both objects and subjects of an embedded clause that are fronted to the beginning of the matrix clause. Neeleman and Titov do not consider local scrambling of the kind discussed here, and its relationship to focus. However, we note that local scrambling is fully compatible with contrastive focus: all of the sentences in (3) can felicitously have the preverbal element in contrastive focus, as shown in (11) for (3a–b).
simplified_text: Some researchers, Neeleman and Titov, think that in Russian, when a word is moved from one sentence to another, it always needs to be reorganized to make sense. This is true for both the subject and object of a sentence that is moved to the beginning of another sentence. They don't talk about a different way of moving words around, called local scrambling, and how it relates to focusing on certain words. However, we found that local scrambling works well with focusing on certain words. For example, all the sentences in (3) can be said with the word at the beginning being the main point, as shown in (11) for (3a-b).
baseline_text: Neeleman and Titov propose that, in Russian, long-distance movement of a contrastively focused element across clause boundaries results in obligatory reconstruction for scope, for both objects and subjects of an embedded clause that are fronted to the beginning of the matrix clause.

complex_text: IV. Head shakes that add meaning. We pass now to consider examples in which the head shake is employed in the course of an utterance in such a way as to express a meaning in addition to any meaning that is being conveyed in words. This can show itself as an expression of some move in addition to the move already being made by the current utterance (as in making a statement and, in addition, answering a question), or as an expression of the speaker’s attitude toward the contents of the verbal component of his utterance.
simplified_text:
Head Shakes That Add Meaning

When we talk, we sometimes use head shakes to add extra meaning to what we're saying. This can happen when we're making a statement and also answering a question, or when we're showing how we feel about what we're saying.

complex_text: The participants in the study were 119 adult native Russian speakers. Participants ranged in age from 18 to 59, with a mean age of 24 and median age of 20. Participants were compensated monetarily for completing the surveys.

complex_text: 8 To distinguish forms of the verb paradigm, we use the feature VFORM of Gazdar et al. 1985. 9 We thus follow Huddleston 1975 in steering a middle course on the question of the relationship between words and word forms. Although we do not necessarily echo Huddleston's decisions with regard to particular forms, we agree with him that we cannot take formal identity to entail word identity; this would mean that there were no cases of ambiguity and no cases of homophony. We also agree with him that formal distinctness in one paradigm cannot be taken to entail word dis- tinctness in all related paradigms, which would mean treating the modal can as three distinct but homophonous words because of the formal distinctness of am/are/is-or even worse, assuming that there are distinct words for each combination of values for features relevant in the language, which would mean treating the modal can as six distinct but homophonous words.
simplified_text: To understand different verb forms, we use a special feature called VFORM. We follow a middle path when thinking about how words and their forms are related. We agree with another expert, Huddleston, that being the same in form doesn't always mean being the same word. This means there can be cases where words sound the same but have different meanings. We also agree with Huddleston that being different in one group of words doesn't always mean being different in all related groups. For example, we can't say that the word "can" has six different meanings just because it has different forms.
baseline_text: We do not necessarily echo Huddleston's decisions with regard to particular forms, we agree with him that we cannot take formal identity to entail word identity; this would mean that there were no cases of ambiguity and no cases of homophony. We also agree with him that formal distinctness in one paradigm cannot be taken to entail word distinctness in all related paradigms, which would mean treating the modal can as three distinct but homophonous words because of the formal distinctness of am/are/is- or even worse, assuming that there are distinct words for each combination of values for features relevant in the language, which would mean treating the modal can as six distinct but homophonous words.
lora_text: We therefore follow Huddleston 1975 in steering a middle course on the question of the relationship between words and word forms.

complex_text: Our study was not designed to tease apart the referential and quantificational readings of odin indefinites, and more empirical work is required on this point (see section 4). However, as discussed above, the comparison between odin and dva indefinites provides evidence for the existence of both readings. On the one hand, the existence of the quantificational readings of indefinites, coupled with a processing-based preference for surface scope, accounts for the similarities in the behavior of odin and dva indefinites. On the other hand, the availability of a referential reading for odin accounts for why odin indefinites are overall accepted less in the distributive context than are dva indefinites.

Our study didn't try to figure out the difference between two kinds of meanings for "some" words. We need more research on this topic. But, as we talked about earlier, comparing "some" words with "both" words shows that both kinds of meanings exist. 

On one hand, the fact that "some" words can have a certain meaning, combined with how our brains process language, explains why "some" and "both" words behave in a similar way. On the other hand, the fact that "some" words can also have a different meaning explains why "some" words are less likely to be used in a certain situation compared to "both" words.

complex_text: We further observe that, to the best of our knowledge, the difference between SVO and OVS orders when it comes to the effects of contrastive focus is something never before noted in the literature on Russian scope. We have reason to believe that this difference would not be revealed via introspection and informal judgments. Our data show that there is quite a bit of variability in speakers’ judgments, and if a researcher relied only on introspection and on gathering informal judgments, it is not clear whether any definitive conclusion could be reached. However, with controlled testing and statistical analysis, the SVO/OVS asymmetry with regard to contrastive focus has proven to be quite robust and has held up across different versions of our test. To conclude, we see this article as a first step in an experimental investigation of how information structure, prosody, and processing preferences influence quantifier scope crosslinguistically.

complex_text: Bailyn argues that rightward extraposition of the subject is theoretically problematic under current syntactic theories, and he proposes yet another derivation of OVS sentences, which requires neither verb movement to T nor right-extraposition of the subject. Bailyn says that when we put the subject at the end of a sentence, it's hard to understand using current rules of grammar. He has a new way to explain these kinds of sentences. This way doesn't need the verb to move to a special spot, nor does it need the subject to move to the end of the sentence. Instead, it works like this: 

(a) We move the object to the front of the sentence, like in example (10a). 

(b) Then, we move the whole group of words that includes the fronted object to a special spot, while the subject stays in its original place, like in example (10b).

For concreteness, associate a context C with a partial function fC from individuals to sets of properties, i. e. a set of of pairs ⟨x, Φ⟩, where x is an individual (or a discourse referent) and Φ is a set of properties. simplified_text:
When we try to understand what a sentence is talking about, we can break it down into two parts. The first part is what the sentence is saying about a specific thing, like a person or an object. We call this part the "pragmatic assertion" of the sentence.

The context of the sentence is also made up of individual things and the properties that are true about them. A good way to think about this is to imagine a stack of index cards. Each card has a name or a description of something, and it also has a list of characteristics or traits that go with it.

Let's use an example to make this clearer. Imagine we're talking about a specific person, like a friend. We can think of the context as a special list that says what's true about that person. This list would have the person's name, and it would also have a list of things that are true about them, like their favorite food or their favorite hobby. So, if we look at the list for that person, it would show us all the things that are true about them.

So for any individual i that is part of the context, fC(i) gives us the set of all properties.

complex_text: The reasons for using dva/two are quite straightforward: to indicate number. By saying that a doctor treated two patients, the speaker is indicating that the doctor is not treating three or four patients. However, this reasoning does not work for odin/one: the singular form of the NP alone, in Russian as well as English, suffices to indicate number. simplified_text: Using dva/two is easy to understand: it shows that something has two things, not three or four. For example, saying a doctor treated two patients means the doctor isn't treating three or four patients. But using odin/one is different. Just saying "one" is enough to show that it's one thing, not two or three. When we hear "one", we look for a reason why it was used. We might think that "one" means a specific person the speaker is talking about. But we don't make that same connection with "two", because saying "two" already tells us that it's not "three" or "four".
